content:
2 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every guide telling you to disable WP-Cron makes the same claim: WordPress fires its pseudo-cron on every page load, and that overhead is dragging your site down.
None of them publishes a number.
We run the servers. So we measured it.
## The three numbers
We audited every WordPress installation on one of our shared hosting servers — a census, not a sample — and timed three operations on the same account, on the same machine, within the same minute.
An empty WP-Cron check: **18 milliseconds**. That’s PHP booting WordPress, reading the scheduled event queue, finding nothing due, and exiting.
The same WordPress boot via WP-CLI: **475 milliseconds**.
An uncached homepage load on that account: **1,540 milliseconds**.
The WP-Cron check is **1.17%** of an uncached page load.
If an uncached page takes 1,540ms and the cron check inside it takes 18ms, then removing WP-Cron addresses roughly one percent of the problem while leaving ninety-nine percent untouched.
If your WordPress site feels slow, WP-Cron is not why.
## The census nobody expects
Across all 26 installations:
**100%** were running WP-Cron in its default configuration.
**0%** had `DISABLE_WP_CRON` set.
**0%** had a replacement server cron.
Across 676 scheduled events — an average of 26 per install — **0%** were overdue.
The failure mode that the entire disable-WP-Cron recommendation exists to prevent was not occurring on a single site we examined.
And 38% of those installations were serving no reachable frontend at all. No traffic. No page loads. WP-Cron depends on page loads to fire. Their overdue count was still zero.
## So when does it actually matter?
There are four conditions where disabling WP-Cron genuinely pays — and none of them is “my site feels slow.” There’s also a specific ordering to the fix that, if you reverse it, silently kills every scheduled task on your site with no error message. And there’s a choice between three cron commands that most guides present as interchangeable, where one consumes a PHP worker from the same pool serving your visitors and one consumes nothing at all.
The full audit — all four data tables, the four conditions, the cPanel walkthrough, and an interactive decision checker that tells you which case you’re in:
**[Read the complete guide on AHosting →](https://www.ahosting.net/blog/disable-wp-cron-measured/)**
